name: HMS Graph (P 715)
events: The history of HMS Graph as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine. Unfortunately there are only logs available for the following months for which full details can be given; 1942; March, August, October, November and December. 1943; January to June.
This page was last updated in December 2019.
29 Sep 1941 U 570 (Lt. G.R. Colvin, RN) departed Hvalfjörður, Iceland for Barrow. She made the passage under her own power escorted by HMS Saladin (Lt.Cdr. L.J. Dover, RN). (1)
3 Oct 1941 U 570 (Lt. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived at Barrow where she was to undergo repairs before a full series of trials could commence. (1)

18 Feb 1942 With her repairs completed HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed from Barrow for Holy Loch to begin a full series of trials on the Scottish west coast. She made the passage together with HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (2)

18 Feb 1942 Around 1345 A/18, P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Barrow for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
Earlier that day, P 43 had conducted compass adjustment trials at Barrow. (3)

24 Feb 1942 HrMs O 10 (Lt. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN), HMS H 43 (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS Graph (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay / Holy Loch for Londonderry. They were escorted by HMS Northern Pride (T/Lt. A.R. Cornish, RNR). (4)
1 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Londonderry for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Hartland (Lt.Cdr. G.P. Billot, RNR). (5)
2 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch. (6)
3 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted gunnery trials in the Clyde area. (6)
4 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HMS Kingfisher (Cdr.(Retd.) W.V.H. Harris, DSC, RN). (6)
5 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HMS Kingfisher (Cdr.(Retd.) W.V.H. Harris, DSC, RN) and HMS ML 199. (6)

7 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted special trials and exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Kingfisher (Cdr.(Retd.) W.V.H. Harris, DSC, RN) and HMS St. Modwen (Lt.Cdr. F.J. Webster, RNR). (6)
8 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (6)
9 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (6)
12 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) returned to Holy Loch from Loch Goil. (6)
13 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted trials in the Clyde area. (6)
14 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted trials in the Clyde area. (6)
22 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (6)
23 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh. (6)
24 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HNoMS Draug. (6)
25 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (6)
26 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area followed by special trials with aircraft. (6)
29 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Boarhound (Skr. S.G. Jinks, RNR). (6)
30 Mar 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) shifted from Holy Loch to Govan where she was immediately docked in No.3 graving dock. (6)
25 Apr 1942 After her fist series of trials HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed from Holy Loch for Portsmouth. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (7)
27 Apr 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth. (2)
29 Apr 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed from Portsmouth for Sheerness. She is to proceed to Chatham dockyard for refit and alterations. (2)
1 May 1942 HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) arrived at Sheerness. She later makes the short trip to Chatham Dockyard. (2)
14 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) ended a refit at Chatham Dockyard. She proceeded to Sheerness. (8)
16 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed from Sheerness for Portsmouth. She is escorted by HMS Vesper (Lt. D.H.R. Bromley, RN). (8)
17 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Portsmouth. (8)
18 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed from Portsmouth for Holy Loch.
At 0955 hours/19 she made rendez-vous with the Free French submarine FFS Rubis and their escort through the Irish Sea, the torpedo boat Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR). (8)
21 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Holy Loch for a period of training before she will be deployed on patrols. (8)
25 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F and full power trials in the Clyde area. (8)
26 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F trials in the Clyde area. (8)
27 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F trials in the Clyde area. (8)
28 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) shifted from Holy Loch to Campbeltown. (8)
29 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F trials off Campbeltown. (8)
30 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F trials off Campbeltown. (8)
31 Aug 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted D/F trials off Campbeltown. (8)
1 Oct 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area. (9)
3 Oct 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area. (9)
7 Oct 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (9)
8 Oct 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) serving as the target.

Upon completion of these HMS Graph departed from Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay. Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN). Escort was provided by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (10)
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Graph during her 1st wa patrol see the map below.

21 Oct 1942 While on patrol in the Bay of Biscay, HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) attacks but misses German U-boat U-333 with four torpedoes about 50 nautical miles north-north-east of Cape Ortegal, Spain in position 44°31'N, 07°25'W.
(All times are zone -1) 1540 hours - HE was reported bearing 290°.

1620 hours - Sighted a four-engined aircraft overhead. It was identified as being most likely a German Focke-Wulf aircraft. Meanwhile the HE had ceased. Up to this moment the HE had been thought to be that of a trawler but it was now considered that it could be a submarine that had dived upon the approach of the aircraft.
1645 hours - Strong HE was now picked up again bearing 300°. Graph was maneuvered onto a firing course.
1652 hours - The conning tower of the enemy was seen for the first time. Started attack.

1658 hours - Fired four torpedoes. Two explosions were heard after about five and a half minutes. These were followed by breaking up noises. (no explanation can be given for the 'breaking up noises' that were heard).
The KTB (Kriegs Tage Buch, logbook) of U-333 gives the following; (All times are zone -2) 1654 hours - Sighted aircraft, could not be identified but it was quite big. Dived.
1737 hours - Surfaced.

1755 hours - Sighted torpedo tracks to Starboard. Looked like one torpedo ran on the surface. Turned hard to Port. Four tracks were seen, all missed astern. Four end of run detonations were heard. Sent report to B.d.U. and cleared the area zig-zagging. (10)

29 Oct 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Holy Loch. Passage north through the Irish Sea had been made togerher with HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN). The submarines had been escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). They had made rendez-vous on the 27th. (10)
14 Nov 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) is docked in AFD 7 at Kames Bay, Cumbrae Island. (11)
15 Nov 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) left dock and returns to Holy Loch. (11)

19 Nov 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed from Holy Loch for her 2nd war patrol. She is to patrol in the Bay of Biscay. During the passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (10)
For the daily positions of HMS Graph during this patrol see the map below.

29 Nov 1942 The Italian blockade breaker Cortellazzo had departed Bordeaux bound for Japan on 29 November 1942. The next day the ship was spotted by a Sunderland flying boat. The British submarines HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), and HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN), were ordered to intercept but failed to do so. (12)
30 Nov 1942 The German tanker Antarktis (10711 GRT, built 1939) left Vigo at 0030 A/30 while the Italian blockade breaker Cortellazzo (5292 GRT, built 1931) was on passage from Bordeaux to Japan.
The Cortellazo was detected at 0400 A/30, by an Allied aircraft in position 44°30'N, 09°18'W, course 230°, speed 10 knots.

The submarines HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN), HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) were patrolling / on passage in the general area.

In a signal timed 0541 A/30, HMS Sealion reported the following to the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.); Unknown ship in position 44°52'N, 10°32'W, course 270°, speed unknown. 82 round per minute were counted. Enemy vessel probably diesel driven [which was correct]. Red Very lights only seen.
At 0654 A/30, another aircraft reported one unidentified motor vessel in position 43°58'N, 09°59'W, course 270°, speed 15 knots.
In a signal timed 0715 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Graph; Total bombing restrictions arranged. If nothing sighted by 1400 A/30 double on your tracks to zone 'K 501'.
At 0725 A/30, an aircraft reported that it had attacked the enemy motor vessel by level bombing. The results were unobserved.
The same aircraft reported at 0950 A/30 that the enemy had been in position 44°25'N, 11°10'W at 0850 A/30. Enemy course was 270°, speed 12 knots.

In a signal timed 1034 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to HMS Clyde and HMS Graph. Estimated position, course and speed of the enemy motor vessel at 0850 A/30, 44°40'N. 11°10'W, 260°, 13 knots. Proceeding forthwith on surface to intercept.
In a signal timed 1241 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; German tanker Antarktis left Vigo at 0030 A/30. HrMs Dolfijn patrol in the vicinity of Cape Ortegal.

In a signal timed 1334 A/30 the F.O.S. sent the following to the submarines; Estimate Antarktis will pass Vallarno between 1100 A/30 and 1200 A/30. Endeavour intercept as far to westward as practicable. At all costs avoid being seen.

In a signal timed 1404 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to the submarines; 1) Liberator aircraft diverted from patrol B.P. 16 to locate and shadow the Cortellazzo. 2) A second Liberator proceeding to position 43°45'N, 07°40'W, thence coastwise westwards to locate the Antarktis.
In a signal timed 1531 A/30, the C-in-C Plymouth, reported the following to the F.O.S.; Cortellazzo in position 44°26'N, 13°38'W, course 260° to 270°, speed 13 knots, at 1415 A/30.
In a signal timed 1547 A/30, the F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; My signal timed 1334 A/30 refers to HrMs Dolfijn only.

In a signal timed 1606 A/30, the F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; Aircraft reported the approximate position, course and speed of the Cortellazzo at 1415 A/30 as 44°36'N, 13°38'E, 260°, 13 knots.
At 1614 A/30, an aircraft reported the following; Have attacked enemy ship at bomb level. Estimate no hits. Position of enemy at 1540 A/30 was 44°46'N, 12°37'W.

In a signal timed 1725 A/30, HMS Clyde reported the following to the F.O.S.; Your 1606 A/30. My position, course and speed are 44°38'N, 14°10'E, 210°, 13 knots. Estimate enemy passing 20 miles ahead. Am searching to westward till dark then will return to original route. Estimate fuel remaining on arrival at Bishop Rock on 4 December 12 hours steaming.

In a signal timed 1832 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Clyde and HMS Graph; l) Estimate enemy furthest on longitude at dawn first December will be 18°45'W. 2) If not in contact with enemy and fuel permits proceed with dispatch towards positions as follows and establish patrol as follows; HMS Clyde, 44°01'N, 18°40'W, HMS Graph, 43°35'n, 18°40'W. 3) Total bombing restrictions extended.

In a signal timed 1952 A/50 the F.O.S. informed HrMs Dolfijn of the following; Estimate the Antarktis can cross 05°30'W at 0730 A/1. If not in contact proceed eastwards at your best speed keeping close outside Spanish territorial waters. Endeavour to remain on the surface until 0830 A/1 but it is essential you are not sighted. Enemy air activity is likely along the route of the Antarktis.

In a signal timed 2026 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Clyde; Your 1725 A/30. 1) My 1832 A/30. Cancel instructions to Clyde. 2) Clyde proceed dived by day by route in my signal timed 1059 A/26, passing through position 46°01'N, 13°22'W at 0800 A/1. (3) Bombing restrictions will follow.

In a signal timed 2341 A/30 the F.O.S. sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn; My 1952 A/30. Amend as follows. Second paragraph proceed at best speed along line 070° from 43°51'N, 07°53'W. Cancel the third paragraph. Continue on the surface as long as practicable. (13)

6 Dec 1942 Around 0100 A/6, HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Falmouth for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN).
Around 0800 A/6, near Bishops Rock, HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) joined company. (14)
8 Dec 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Holy Loch. (10)
19 Dec 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (15)

21 Dec 1942 Around 1130 A/21, the British submarines HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN), HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and the Dutch submarine HrMs O 14 (Lt.Cdr. H.A.W. Goossens, RNN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick. They were escorted by the British ASW trawler HMS Lord Austin (T/Lt. E.L. Wathen, RNR). (15)
23 Dec 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Lerwick. (15)
24 Dec 1942 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed from Lerwick for her 3rd war patrol. She is to patrol off Northern Norway to provide cover for convoy JW-51 to Northern Russia.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Graph during this patrol see the map below.
1 Jan 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) makes a torpedo attack on two 'destroyers' off the Altafjord. All torpedoes fired missed their intended targets.

(All times are zone -1) 0106 hours - In position 70°51'N, 21°56'E sighted a darkened vessel probably a cruiser. Distance was 12000 to 15000 yards. Altered course to attack. The ship was however lost after about 20 minutes.

0431 hours - In position 70°48'N, 21°20'E sighted a destroyer bearing 240°. Range was about 7000 - 8000 yards. The enemy appeared to be steering from left to right. A second destroyer was sighted almost immediately behind the first. Their courses appeared to be very erratic and their speeds were very low. They were thought to be carrying out an A/S search. Turned towards to attack.

0440 hours - Fired four torpedoes at the destroyers. Two heavy explosions were heard 7 and 7 1/2 minutes after firing. Two more, much smaller, explosions were heard 9 minutes after firing. Graph retired on the surface after firing. The results were not observed.

The ships sighted by HMS Graph were the German auxiliary A/S trawlers UJ 1103 and UJ 1109 that were on an A/S patrol. The log of UJ 1103 / Gustav Körner reported one strong underwater explosion was heard and after minute one more. The ship was shaken, but not damaged. Time was 0450 hours. (10)

3 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1641 A/3, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) leave patrol 1600 A/4th for Lerwick passing through following positions; A) Graph, 70°22'N, 12°45'E, thence by route 'Pink'. B) P 49, 70°54'N, 16°40'E, thence by route 'Orange'. C) P 312, 71°10'N, 16°02'E, thence by route 'Black'. D) P 216, 71°26'N, 17°00'E, thence by route 'Black'. (16)

5 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1814 A/5, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN); After crossing latitude 68°00'N submarines are to proceed dived by day as follows; Graph to the vicinity of position 64°35'N, 09°44'E. P 312 to the vicinity of position 64°44'N, 09°26'E. This was later corrected to 64°55'N, 09°56'E in the F.O.S.'s signal timed 1651 A/6. P 49 to the vicinity of position 66°42'N, 11°56'E. P 216 to the vicinity of position 66°56'N, 12°01'E.
The Admiralty expected the German battleship Tirpitz to proceed from Trondheim to Narvik. (16)

6 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1134 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN); 1) I appreciate that enemy main units may be moving to the Norwegian coast shortly. 2) Object of submarines on patrol off the Norwegian coast are cruiser(s) and above but submarines except P 312 may attack U-Boats if definitely identified as hostile. One full salvo is to be retained for main units. 3) If force retires at any time S/Ms have freedom of action as follows; P 216 north and P 49 south of 66°50'N, P 312 north and Graph south of 64°45'N. 4) Landfalls have been satisfactorily made in past as follows; (A) Röst, (B) Myken, (C) Sklindenbank, (D) Nordoerne. 5) Submarines are not to enter following areas unless ordered by F.O.S. 6) Submerged bombing restrictions have been arranged in patrol areas until further notice.

In a signal timed 1727 A/6, these submarines were informed that they were not to be on the surface within 30 miles from position 64°45'N, 09°49'E from 0800 A/7 until further notice and within 30 miles from position 66°50'N, 11°57'E from 0100 A/7 until further notice. (16)
13 Jan 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Lerwick. (10)
14 Jan 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed from Lerwick bound for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Qualicum (T/Lt. F.S. Tolliday, RNR). (17)
16 Jan 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Holy Loch. She is used for training duties from now on. (17)
7 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted main motor trials in Loch Goil followed by D/G trials in Gare Loch. (18)
8 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (18)
9 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (18)
10 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. Upon completion HMS Graph proceeded to Holy Loch. (18)
20 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (18)
25 Feb 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted speed trials in the Clyde area. (18)

25 Feb 1943 HMS Usurper (Lt. D.R.O. Mott, DSC, RN) was conducting exercises on Inmarnock Water when she was ordered to search in the Clyde area for her missing sister ship HMS Vandal (Lt. J.S. Bridger, RN).

The following submarines / ships were also ordered to search for HMS Vandal (which was not found until 1994); HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, DSO, RN), HMS L 27 (Lt. W.N. Eade, RNR), HMS Severn (Lt.Cdr. A.N.G. Campbell, RN), HMS Stubborn (Lt. A.A. Duff, RN), HMS Whitehall (Cdr. C.L.de H. Bell, RD, RNR), HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN), HMS Kingfisher (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) G.H. Gandy, RN), HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR), HMS Cygnet (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) F.B. Proudfoot, RN), HMS Tedworth (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) G.H. Warren, RN) and HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN). (19)
2 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) conducted sound trials in Loch Long. (20)
6 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed Holy Loch for Londonderry. (20)
7 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Londonderry. (20)
8 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Lough Foyle. (20)
9 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Lough Foyle. (20)
10 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Lough Foyle. (20)
11 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Lough Foyle. (20)
12 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Lough Foyle. (20)
20 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) departed Londonderry for Holy Loch. (20)
21 Mar 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) arrived at Holy Loch. (20)
3 Apr 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) shifted from Holy Loch to Arrochar where se conducted torpedo firing trials at the torpedo range. (21)
4 Apr 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted torpedo firing trials at the torpedo range off Arrochar. (21)
5 Apr 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted torpedo firing trials at the torpedo range off Arrochar. Upon completion of these HMS Graph proceeded to Holy Loch. (21)
9 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) departed from Holy Loch for Barrow. (22)
10 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) arrived at Barrow. (22)
19 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) departed from Barrow for Holy Loch. (22)
20 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) arrived at Holy Loch. (22)
26 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) shifted from Holy Loch to Rothesay. (22)
27 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HMS Saltburn (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.D. Parker, RNR). (22)
28 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area. (22)
29 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area. (22)
30 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HMS Saltburn (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.D. Parker, RNR). (22)
31 May 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area with HMS Saltburn (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.D. Parker, RNR). (22)
1 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) conducted special trials in the Clyde area. Upon completion of these HMS Graph proceeded to Holy Loch. (23)
5 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) departed from Holy Loch for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (2)
8 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) arrived at Dundee. (2)
10 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) departed from Dundee bound for Chatham. She was escorted by HMS Castlenau (T/Skr. T. Baxter, RNR). (2)
12 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) arrived at Sheerness. (2)
13 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) departs Sheerness ad arrived at Chatham shortly afterwards. (2)
21 Jun 1943 HMS Graph (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) is put into dockyard control at the Chatham Dockyard. (2)
